title: Wait Thou Only Upon God
subtitle: My soul, wait thou only upon God

verses.text: Truly my soul waiteth upon God: from him cometh my salvation.
He only is my rock and my salvation; he is my defence; I shall not be greatly moved.
How long will ye imagine mischief against a man? ye shall be slain all of you: as a bowing wall shall ye be, and as a tottering fence.
They only consult to cast him down from his excellency: they delight in lies: they bless with their mouth, but they curse inwardly. Selah.
My soul, wait thou only upon God; for my expectation is from him.
He only is my rock and my salvation: he is my defence; I shall not be moved.
In God is my salvation and my glory: the rock of my strength, and my refuge, is in God.
Trust in him at all times; ye people, pour out your heart before him: God is a refuge for us. Selah.
Surely men of low degree are vanity, and men of high degree are a lie: to be laid in the balance, they are altogether lighter than vanity.
Trust not in oppression, and become not vain in robbery: if riches increase, set not your heart upon them.
God hath spoken once; twice have I heard this; that power belongeth unto God.
Also unto thee, O Lord, belongeth mercy: for thou renderest to every man according to his work.

context: A meditation of David on exclusive trust amid enemies who plot to topple him ("a bowing wall… a tottering fence"), likely from a season of political instability. Henry stresses the repeated word "only"—God alone as rock, salvation, and refuge—as the psalm's central discipline against divided trust.

verse_commentary.note: "Truly my soul waiteth upon God: from him cometh my salvation. He only is my rock and my salvation." Enemies seek to destroy him "as a bowing wall," delighting in lies and blessing with the mouth while cursing inwardly—instability all around, met by settled trust.

He preaches to his own soul: "My soul, wait thou only upon God... He is my rock... Trust in him at all times; ye people, pour out your heart before him." Public exhortation follows private discipline—trust is meant to be modeled, not only privately held.

"Men of low degree are vanity, and men of high degree are a lie... trust not in oppression... if riches increase, set not your heart upon them." Power belongs to God, and to Him alone belongs mercy—closing with the double truth heard "twice," that strength and steadfast love both come from Him.

meaning: The word "only" is the psalm's hinge: salvation, rock, and trust must rest exclusively in God—not divided among human status, oppression's advantage, or increasing riches—because power and mercy both belong solely to Him.
application: Notice where you quietly split trust between God and something else and practice saying "he only" specifically about that thing. Preach truth to your own soul as David does (v. 5), rather than waiting for feelings to catch up. When riches or influence increase, deliberately "set not your heart upon them," redirecting gratitude back to God's power and mercy.
questions: Where in your life is trust currently divided rather than resting "only" upon God?
What would it look like this week to "pour out your heart before him" as verse 8 urges?
If your riches or influence increased, how would you guard against setting your heart on them (v. 10)?
sources: Matthew Henry, Commentary on the Whole Bible (themes summarized)
Expository themes consistent with MacArthur Study Bible tradition (paraphrased, not quoted)
